video_title: SBF, Gensler, Warren and Crypto’s War for Washington
gist: Wintermute policy head Ron Hammond maps the 2026 state of crypto-in-Washington: the Clarity Act market-structure bill is in final agriculture/banking merge with a self-handicapped ~45% pass odds, the bank lobby has pivoted from attacking stablecoin yield to attacking AML to kill the bill, Warren/Gensler-era hostility has faded as Democrats break ranks, and the next fights are crypto tax, DeFi, and self-custody/privacy. Recurring edge: DC narratives are manufactured via proxy groups and planted letters, so on-chain/Twitter transparency now lets you front-run regulatory catalysts.

insights.claim: The Clarity Act (crypto market-structure bill) is in final reconciliation of its agriculture and banking committee versions, with the contested language being 'ancillary asset' definitions where two-or-three-word changes carry large legal-classification consequences.
insights.speaker: Ron Hammond (Wintermute head of policy)
insights.so_what: How 'ancillary asset' is defined determines whether tokens fall under SEC or CFTC jurisdiction — watch the merged text for which assets get commodity treatment.
insights.quote: there's differences of like ancillary asset definitions and like all these are kind of small you know two or three words but they mean like the world for the legal world

insights.claim: Senator Lummis wants a Clarity Act vote announced before July 4th and held before end of July, putting a best-case presidential signing around September 2026.
insights.speaker: Ron Hammond
insights.so_what: A concrete legislative calendar — a July vote announcement is the near-term catalyst to watch for crypto market-structure clarity.
insights.quote: the goal from Lumis is that she wants a vote announced before July 4th and then have a vote before end of July which would end up in the best case scenario being like September time frame

insights.claim: Hammond puts Clarity Act passage odds at ~45%, explicitly below Galaxy's Alex Thorne's 70% estimate.
insights.speaker: Ron Hammond
insights.so_what: An insider lobbyist is more bearish than a sell-side voice — a coin-flip on passage means hedging, not pricing in clarity as a base case.
insights.quote: I'm at 45%. I know Alex Thorne went on your podcast said 70%. I'm at 45.

insights.claim: If the Clarity Act fails in 2026, crypto market-structure law likely slips to 2028–2030 because it almost certainly won't advance with Democrats controlling either chamber.
insights.speaker: Ron Hammond
insights.so_what: The 2026 window is binary — a miss means a multi-year regulatory vacuum, raising the stakes of the July vote for US-listed crypto names.
insights.quote: if it doesn't happen this year, it could be like 2028, 2030 um when this would get get done

insights.claim: The Clarity Act now has crypto market-structure provisions entangled with executive-branch ethics provisions aimed at Trump's crypto conflicts, a non-crypto fight that could stall the bill.
insights.speaker: Ron Hammond
insights.so_what: Bill risk is now driven by ethics/corruption politics rather than crypto policy itself — passage could hinge on Trump-ethics negotiations, not token rules.
insights.quote: now we're taking a crypto regulatory bill and we're throwing in a bunch of ethic provisions on the executive branch

insights.claim: Both sides are softening on the Clarity Act ethics fight: the Trump camp moved from 'no ethics whatsoever' to openness to dialogue with Democrats, but no actual text proposals exist yet.
insights.speaker: Ron Hammond
insights.so_what: Softening signals a negotiated path is possible; absence of text means the deal is unconfirmed — watch for the first ethics-language draft as the real signal.
insights.quote: the Trump camp went from like no ethics whatsoever to saying we are open to having dialogue with Democrats to find a path forward

insights.claim: The bank lobby has conceded the stablecoin-yield fight — the yield amendment is locked and can't get 60 Senate votes to change — and pivoted to attacking the bill on AML/BSA grounds to kill it entirely.
insights.speaker: Ron Hammond
insights.so_what: Stablecoin yield is effectively safe; the live threat to the bill is now the AML narrative, so watch whether banks' 'no AML' claim gains traction despite ~70 pages of AML text.
insights.quote: the banks have now pivoted from the yield issue because they know they've lost that issue... now Jamie Diamond, all these other CEOs are saying, "Oh, there's an AML issue."

insights.claim: Jamie Dimon publicly criticized the Clarity Act over the weekend, signaling JPMorgan is leading the bank pushback as the bill nears a vote.
insights.speaker: Ron Hammond
insights.so_what: Top-bank-CEO opposition is the institutional counter-lobby to track; Dimon's framing previews the AML kill-the-bill narrative.
insights.quote: Jamie Diamond, we just crap on the Clarity Act this weekend

insights.claim: The crypto lobby has grown from ~4 lobbyists in 2016-17 to roughly 200 today, still far smaller than the bank lobby but repeatedly out-maneuvering it.
insights.speaker: Ron Hammond
insights.so_what: Quantifies how fast crypto's DC footprint scaled — a structural tailwind for favorable legislation despite being outspent.
insights.quote: when I started... 2016 2017 or four lobbyists maybe right... and now I think it's probably 200 probably. It's still a lot smaller than the banks have but... we've beaten the banks at their own game several times

insights.claim: Banks use a 'rural community bank will die' fearmongering frame against stablecoin yield (e.g. small-town Nebraska losing all lending), which Hammond characterizes as empirically baseless BS.
insights.speaker: Ron Hammond
insights.so_what: Recognize the community-bank-collapse argument as a rhetorical device, not a real disintermediation forecast, when assessing stablecoin-yield headline risk.
insights.quote: that mom and pop bank that's keep your town alive in rural Nebraska um if yield goes through that small town Nebraska won't have any lending whatsoever... which is complete BS

insights.claim: Opposition to crypto bills is frequently laundered through unrelated proxy groups — housing groups, AFL-CIO, and progressive orgs came out against the Clarity Act despite not being affected by it, plausibly fronting for banks like Wells Fargo.
insights.speaker: Ron Hammond
insights.so_what: When 'grassroots' opposition to a crypto bill appears, trace the funding — the visible opponent is often a proxy for an incumbent financial interest.
insights.quote: We had like a bunch of housing groups the AFL CIO and a bunch other random progressive groups come out against the bill even though in the most part if not all the times like they're not touched by it

insights.claim: Lawmakers' attack letters are often ghostwritten verbatim by interested parties; Hammond cites a short-seller (Marc Cohodes) who shopped a pre-drafted letter on Silicon Valley Bank / Silvergate that Warren then read 'word for word' shortly before the bank collapsed.
insights.speaker: Ron Hammond
insights.so_what: A regulatory/political letter can be a short thesis in disguise — treat senator letters that precede a price collapse as potential trade signals, not neutral oversight.
insights.quote: she led the letter like word for word from the short seller on Silicon Valley Bank and then what like two weeks ago or afterwards it like goes under

insights.claim: Allegation-based letters with no evidence can trigger investigations that generate headlines sufficient to short an asset; Hammond cites ICE and CME writing the CFTC and Agriculture committees claiming Hyperliquid 'could be involved' in sanctions violations and money laundering.
insights.speaker: Ron Hammond
insights.so_what: An evidence-free 'could be involved' letter from incumbent exchanges is a competitive weapon — Hyperliquid faces headline/investigation risk engineered by ICE/CME rivals.
insights.quote: ICE and CME had a letter on Hyperlid two weeks ago... Hyperlid could be involved could be involved in sanctions violation and money laundering. Again, they didn't have any evidence

insights.claim: Regulators plant narratives in Wall Street Journal / Financial Times to pre-justify new authority requests (e.g. 'North Korea uses crypto' ahead of a Treasury sanction-authority proposal), so adverse mainstream articles often precede a regulatory action.
insights.speaker: Ron Hammond
insights.so_what: A sudden negative WSJ/FT crypto piece can be a leading indicator of an imminent Treasury/OFAC action — useful as an early-warning signal for sanction-authority risk.
insights.quote: they go to Wall Street Journal Financial Times... it's usually like a plant from a regulator or interest party... North Korea is using crypto... oh and by the way, Treasury tomorrow is going to propose new authority

insights.claim: On-chain and Twitter transparency now lets observers identify who is behind a lobbying attack (e.g. exposing a letter's author as a short seller), creating an information edge versus the pre-social-media era.
insights.speaker: Ron Hammond
insights.so_what: Doing source diligence on who is pushing a regulatory narrative is now a tradable edge — the manipulator's identity is often discoverable before the market reacts.
insights.quote: now you go on Twitter and it's like who's like Mark Hodis it's like oh this guy's a short seller this guy's probably going to make a lot of money on this stuff

insights.claim: Elizabeth Warren's political power has measurably declined: when she physically posts at the Senate vote desk, the first 2-3 Democrats to defy her now trigger ~15 more to follow, a herding dynamic that broke her former deterrence.
insights.speaker: Ron Hammond
insights.so_what: Warren can no longer reliably whip Democratic votes against crypto bills — reduces a key downside risk to pro-crypto legislation passing.
insights.quote: the second that like two or three senators and Democrats side pass Liza Warren sign their vote, you notice about 15 more come afterwards

insights.claim: Warren and the anti-crypto Democrats were primarily 'carrying Gary Gensler's water,' and Gensler's SEC took dinners and multiple meetings with FTX, giving FTX special access relative to other firms.
insights.speaker: Ron Hammond
insights.so_what: Recasts the prior enforcement regime as personality/vendetta-driven and compromised — context for why the policy reversal under Trump has been so sharp.
insights.quote: they were mostly carrying Gary Gensler's water here... he was taking dinners with FTX he was doing multiple meetings with FTX

insights.claim: The SEC's suit against Coinbase was timed to land the day before Coinbase testified to the House Agriculture Committee, which Hammond reads as a deliberate political message rather than a legal necessity.
insights.speaker: Ron Hammond
insights.so_what: Illustrates how enforcement timing was weaponized for narrative — a cautionary template for how a future hostile SEC could disrupt a company's DC moment.
insights.quote: the SEC sued Coinbase the day of they were testifying in front of House Agriculture Committee and that was so political. The timing was so nefarious

insights.claim: Prometheum functioned as essentially a Gensler/SEC-aligned front in hearings — its witness read talking points lifted directly from Gensler and Maxine Waters' staff — and the company is now effectively irrelevant in the industry.
insights.speaker: Ron Hammond
insights.so_what: The 'compliant counter-example' company strategy failed and discredited itself — a reminder that regulator-endorsed industry tokens lacked durable legitimacy.
insights.quote: every talking point is directly from Gary Gensler. It's directly from Maxine Waters staff... I think they're irrelevant at this point

insights.claim: Warren will not pivot on crypto under any circumstances, and DC now broadly reads her as reflexively 'anti-something' rather than for anything.
insights.speaker: Ron Hammond
insights.so_what: Don't expect any crypto détente from Warren personally; her opposition is a fixed, increasingly discounted constant in vote math.
insights.quote: she's still she she doesn't pivot. She never does.

insights.claim: If Democrats retake the Senate, committee chairs would be Elizabeth Warren and Maxine Waters, and because Democrats have no committee-chair term limits (unlike the GOP's 6-year cap) these gatekeepers could block crypto bills indefinitely.
insights.speaker: Ron Hammond
insights.so_what: The single biggest structural risk to crypto legislation is a Democratic chamber flip handing agenda control to Warren/Waters — a macro-political variable to weight in 2026/2028 positioning.
insights.quote: Democrats you can be in those seats for life... if they win it be Alyssa Warren and Maxine Waters

insights.claim: Sherrod Brown, the anti-crypto 'lieutenant' to Warren who killed a stablecoin bill three years ago (despite Senator Toomey's sign-off) and lost his seat in 2024, is running again as newly 'pro-crypto' — a pivot Hammond explicitly distrusts.
insights.speaker: Ron Hammond
insights.so_what: A Brown win would make him Senate Agriculture chairman alongside Warren — his election-cycle pro-crypto conversion is a credibility risk, not a reliable ally signal.
insights.quote: I don't trust post pivot during election cycle while potentially trying to court crypto money

insights.claim: Newer-generation Democrats are breaking ranks to admit the party 'screwed up' on crypto under Gensler in 2024, signaling a generational shift away from the old anti-crypto guard.
insights.speaker: Ron Hammond
insights.so_what: A long-term tailwind: younger Democrats becoming crypto-tolerant erodes the durability of the anti-crypto bloc even if the old guard retains chairs near-term.
insights.quote: the Democrats on the newer side... like hey like we we kind of screwed up here um on the crypto side in 2024 underneath Gensler

insights.claim: Crypto's electoral wins in 2024 (via Fairshake and allied PACs) materially affected races and are now actively deterring politicians in 2026 from opposing the industry.
insights.speaker: Ron Hammond
insights.so_what: Crypto PAC money is a demonstrated electoral force — a standing deterrent that supports favorable legislative odds independent of any single bill.
insights.quote: credit to Fairshake and all these other groups that really stepped up in 2024... we had material effects in the 2024 election that's now scaring a lot of people right now in 2026

insights.claim: The Saudi investment angle into Trump's World Liberty Financial reframed his crypto conflicts as a legitimate national-security concern that 'kickstarted everything,' and is more damaging than the earlier memecoin story.
insights.speaker: Ron Hammond
insights.so_what: The Saudi/WLF national-security narrative is the live political liability dragging on the Clarity Act — distinct from generic 'crypto is scammy' attacks and harder to dismiss.
insights.quote: it's more the hook of like Saudi uh investment getting involved. Like that story that dropped a couple months ago like that really kickstarted everything

insights.claim: Trump-affiliated crypto ventures (the Trump memecoin, World Liberty Financial) involve a 'whole new cast of characters' outside the established Coinbase/Kraken/Binance policy world and will draw heavy hearing scrutiny if Democrats take a chamber.
insights.speaker: Ron Hammond
insights.so_what: Headline/hearing risk is concentrated in Trump-adjacent crypto entities, not the mainstream exchanges — useful for distinguishing idiosyncratic political risk from sector-wide risk.
insights.quote: it wasn't the people that we dealt with like you know Kraken or Coinbase or Binance... there's just a whole new like cast of characters

insights.claim: Hammond assesses that Trump's crypto conflicts have damaged the broader industry's reputation less than he feared, with most Republicans still solidly pro-crypto.
insights.speaker: Ron Hammond
insights.so_what: Contains the contagion thesis — Trump's conflicts are being read as Trump-specific rather than indicting crypto, supporting continued GOP legislative support.
insights.quote: actually it's not as bad as I thought it' be

insights.claim: Democrats may try to extend a single bad actor's sins to the whole industry — e.g. arguing that because World Liberty Financial got an OCC charter, no other crypto firm should be granted one.
insights.speaker: Ron Hammond
insights.so_what: Crypto bank-charter access is a contested post-Clarity battleground — watch for WLF being used as the wedge to freeze OCC charters industry-wide.
insights.quote: if Worldly Financial got an OCC charter, no one else in crypto should get another OCC charter

themes.theme: Next battlegrounds: tax, DeFi, self-custody, privacy

insights.claim: Crypto tax provisions — mark-to-market for digital-asset exchanges and a de minimis exemption — are advancing, with a Ways and Means hearing imminent, though final enactment likely waits for the next tax bill.
insights.speaker: Ron Hammond
insights.so_what: A de minimis exemption and mark-to-market treatment would materially change US crypto usage and trading economics — track the Ways and Means hearing and the next tax bill.
insights.quote: things like Litecoin exchange mark to market dimminimus like these are huge T proposals... the next tax bill is we're going to see a tax provision there on crypto. It will be huge.

insights.claim: DeFi regulation is the next frontier nobody has solved: the US deliberately deferred it to do centralized-exchange rules first, while the EU and UK are now attempting DeFi rules and will likely 'mess it up.'
insights.speaker: Ron Hammond
insights.so_what: DeFi remains a regulatory open question globally — first-mover EU/UK rules could set adverse precedents; an unresolved area where US framing is still up for grabs.
insights.quote: the EU is now trying to tackle DeFi regulation, so they're going to probably mess it up

insights.claim: Self-custody protection is becoming a defined policy fight — Warren Davidson has made it his core mantra — because self-custody has been targeted by both Republicans and Democrats.
insights.speaker: Ron Hammond
insights.so_what: Self-custody is an under-the-radar but bipartisan-contested issue — a regulatory risk vector for wallets and non-custodial infrastructure worth monitoring.
insights.quote: self custody has been about my old my old boss Warren Davidson like that's now his new mantra has been self-custody protection

insights.claim: Privacy is gaining ground via a 'horseshoe' coalition where the most progressive and most conservative members align against national-security hawks, with banks drifting to the Democratic/hawk side and Republicans toward the pro-privacy tech side.
insights.speaker: Ron Hammond
insights.so_what: Privacy coalitions are realigning along an unexpected horseshoe — a structural opening for privacy-preserving crypto tech, though the security-hawk bloc remains larger.
insights.quote: the most progressives and the most conservatives. They really do meet in the middle on privacy of all issues and they're against the national security hawks

insights.claim: Congress may be more amenable to financial privacy than the executive agencies (Treasury, OFAC), because regulators are institutionally incentivized to maximize data collection while representatives answer to a public that is fatigued with surveillance.
insights.speaker: Ron Hammond
insights.so_what: Privacy advocacy may find more traction in Congress than at agencies — but Congress 'folds a lot' after any bad incident, so the path is fragile.
insights.quote: the regulators like you have more incentive to get as much data as possible... But the American people are kind of done with it

insights.claim: The crypto lobby has consolidated into a unified front — L1 competitors, DeFi, exchanges, market makers and token projects now coordinate in DC despite competing in the market — making it costly for any single actor to defect and cut a self-serving deal, as SBF tried.
insights.speaker: Ron Hammond
insights.so_what: Industry unity is a durable structural strength that raised crypto's legislative win rate — defection (SBF/FTX-style carve-outs) is now socially and politically punished.
insights.quote: there's an L1 group and it's like all the L1 lobbyists from all across spectrum are all coordinating like hey what's good for us is good for us entirely and we'll compete about it outside DC

insights.claim: Wintermute, despite the common 'we'll leave the US' lobbying threat, actually expanded into the US after Trump won, calling it the number-one market — a revealed preference contradicting industry exit rhetoric.
insights.speaker: Ron Hammond
insights.so_what: Firms are voting with their feet toward the US post-Trump — supports the thesis that favorable US policy is pulling crypto liquidity onshore.
insights.quote: they actually operate United States and they came to United States after Trump won saying like, hey, like this is the number one market. We're here to play.

insights.claim: The UK is reversing course on Biden-era-style 'owner' regulations after recognizing firms are leaving, and is now actively trying to onshore crypto firms and undo rules it newly deems bad.
insights.speaker: Ron Hammond
insights.so_what: Regulatory competition is intensifying — a UK turnaround to retain firms signals jurisdictional arbitrage opportunities and a softer UK stance worth tracking.
insights.quote: they've kind of recognized everyone's leaving the UK... they're now saying like, "Oh, we realize these are bad now... By the way, don't leave the UK, please."

insights.claim: Hammond frames competitive battles as increasingly fought through proxies across sectors — citing an 'anthropic battle' in AI run entirely through proxies and the American Gaming Association going through the Mormon church to fight prediction markets.
insights.speaker: Ron Hammond
insights.so_what: Proxy-war lobbying is a cross-sector pattern (AI, gaming, prediction markets) — applies the same who's-really-behind-this diligence to non-crypto policy fights affecting adjacent assets.
insights.quote: the American Gaming Association has been going through the Mormon church to go against prediction markets
